segments.text: Should the dean be bound by the majority vote of the department heads?
Oh, no, no.
It's the same type thing.
You don't want a weak dean.
In fact, I'd say that with pages.
You want a dean that will not only stand up to his department heads
but stand up to the upper administration.
And I think anybody like that has got to be willing to step down any time.
I mean, there are some things that are worth that.
Looking back now over half century almost, well, half century,
can you think of some opportunities that we at A&M in engineering that we missed, we passed by?
Well, one, well, there's a couple.
Well, one of the things I wanted to do, and I couldn't get Prescott to help,
and I couldn't support it, and I believe he was, yeah, he was up there then.
Well, there's a polymer group, and we should be strong in polymers at Texas A&M
because this whole Gulf Coast is in the business of making polymers.
I don't care how many Nobel people type you got.
We need some people working on the chemistry that's going on in Texas.
And I don't think we've got very much of that going on.
We need, that's one of the things that we still need.
That's still an opportunity.
But the whole group wanted to move down here.
From where?
Massachusetts.
The University of Massachusetts, I recall.
And we had it all lined up, Benson and I, and we couldn't get support for it.
Another one I think the university missed,
they're always trying to bring people into this system.
One of the schools that they didn't bring in,
they should have been on the bus.
They were on the ball.
And I told Thompson so.
I think it was too late.
It was Lamar Tech.
Lamar Tech is an engineering school like A&M, a good location on the coast.
And is a school that fits with A&M better.
It got into this, I told him, even though it's in the other bunch,
it doesn't have to stay in there.
The people in Lamar Tech would much prefer to be in our system than in theirs
because there's more to be gained.
We've got a lot of synergisms that you don't have.
with a bunch of teacher schools.
They got in with Sam Houston and some others.
But there's one opportunity that we've missed there,
and they've gotten in a lot of others
that I don't know whether there were opportunities or not.
They may go the other way, but that's one
that ought to be pursued now.
Did we miss on any scholars that you can think of,
any faculty that we might have gotten?
Oh, I don't know that we did.
I'm not sure that we might have offered the one or two
that we didn't get.
But you can't think of any that became really great.
Would have made the difference.
One of the things that I think that another thing that I think
is a real issue is that universities
want to buy their standing.
They go around hiring everybody else's people.
I think a real credit to the university
is did we grow people?
And I think that there's no mark ever given for how many people
did you grow on your faculty, bringing them up
from assistant professor to where they became people
of stature.
And that really should be the mark of a university is,
did you grow them yourself, or did you just put the money out
and get them?
Anybody can buy anything if they got the money.
Who's the old boy that's got CNN?
He can buy it.
But I think there's not enough.
There's not enough credit given, and I don't ever hear
that mentioned, is did you develop these people?
Now, in a company, that's a real mark.
They don't go and get their people from somebody else.
Did you ever have, or do you now have, any department of
chemical engineering elsewhere after whom you wanted us to be
modeled?
No.
I don't think we ought to be looking at somebody else.
That's the trouble.
What we ought to do is look at the state of Texas, the industry
that we have, and then model ourselves to serve the people
that we are set up to serve.
And I think trying to be like somebody else, unless they've
got the same situation we are, we are unique, we've got the A&M
in the name, and we ought to go ahead and pull through on these
other things.
Yeah.
Like your alma mater at NC State, they are very good in
textile.
Yeah.
Well, that was a whole area, textile.
That fits with that state.
It fits with the state.
Yeah.
And we hear a downgrade in agriculture some.
I think that, I was talking to one of the people in agriculture,
and I think his old Harry was wanting to say, well, you don't
need all this.
One guy said, well, hell, I guess you'd get one cow and one
hog and teach agriculture.
But I think agriculture is a big part of it.
I think agriculture is a big part of it.
I think agriculture is a big thing, and it still is a big
thing in the state, and will continue to be.
It may be a different type of agriculture, it may be very
intensive type, but you've got all the different type
industries that we've got here, and we need to be, we've got the
electronic industry and so on, but we've also got the big
chemical industry, and we'll still, this chemical industry is
not going away.
In fact, I'm writing an article right here now, a study that we
did.
It's saying that actually the 20th and 21st century should be
called the chemical age, because you can't look anywhere and see
any material that wasn't in some way touched by the chemical
industry.
You might say, well, the information age and all of this.
Well, actually, that's just come from a calculator that's made
out of chips, which is a chemical product.
So all of those are really subsets of the chemical.
What we've really done is bring on all these new materials that
have made life a lot better, and have made possible all these other
things.
They look like they're the thing that's driving it, and they do
have an upper hand right now, but you've got to have all these
things, and people are not going to do without them, and they're
still going to be coming, more and more of them, and what's
happening is that the life sciences is really, they're
recognizing that they're just chemistry, and as they learn the
chemistry and cancer and all of this, they're going to be able to
better understand and treat all these things as well as the
chemicals produce better foods.
So some of these companies are separating themselves into life
sciences and chemicals, and I think that's a mistake because one of these
days they're going to need a life, what they might refer to as a
biological process, but all they're doing is carrying out a chemical
reaction right next to another one that they regard as a chemical
reaction.
So I think that's the direction that we're going to go in.
They'll come back together.
Right now they're going apart, but they'll come back together.
And so I see the chemical as being a big thing from now on.
Am I correct that you appear to be defining chemical engineering as applied
chemistry?
Chemistry in the real world that serves a purpose?
Yeah.
Let's see, as you went out to either search for funding for chemical engineering
research or to work more on chemical engineering, what did you do?
I did a lot of research.
I did a lot of research.
I worked more closely with some of our alumni and so forth.
Do you have any stories of the real world of how those people felt about A&M and about
the chemical engineering here?
Oh, I'm not, it's been a long time since I've, I did go with them
the other day though, and old Charles Herter, just a surprising thing.
I taught him, and it's a good thing I continued to teach because he graduated around the same
time I graduated, around 72.
And you know, Les Appelt, he is now the president and chief executive officer of that company.
And he was a chemical engineer, and the reason he's done so well is that he can judge commercial
projects.
And so he's at the very top of it, and I went with old Legetti and him down there to hunt
some money.
And I hadn't really had much to do with him since he graduated.
I'd met him once other time.
And, you know, he was a good guy.
He was a good guy.
He was a good guy.
He was a good guy.
And when J.D.
called up, he said, be sure and bring Dr. Holland with you.
And we got down there, and he said, oh, he just welcomed with Omar, and he said, can
I give you a hug?
And I was sure glad that, even though I could have just sat on my butt and not taught anything
as department head, I continued to teach because those are the kind of relationships that you,
you don't realize how many of them are out there until you get out there.
We had a big class at the end of the M.T.
and we were able to pay for a whole class on the 5th of August.
And I figured, since we had just started, I could do whatever I wanted.
But I wanted to stay out of the classroom.
So I had never really found any students that were interested in teaching the math The
internet was a big thing.
I could learn something new.
It's hard to do a few things a day without talking to people.
Because they're scattered all over.
Let's see.
Did you ever have, you know, I talked with this old boy that wasn't even one of our students.
The boy, the guy that owned Petr-Tex that bought it and just made millions of dollars.
55, 56, down at Dow.
And he told me that when he saw that I was going to give the talk,
he said, I don't think that could be the same Dr. Holland that I knew
and taught me that course.
But now he's an old man.
Well, he said that at that time he was about 20, 21, and I was about 30.
And he thought that that looked awfully old to him then, 30 years old.
But you run across, I've run across a lot of people
that I've taught in graduate extension courses.
So I think, actually, well, you go back to the Bible.
What was the teacher?
And this is what people don't think about,
that teaching probably is the most honorable of all professions
and the most important.
And it's the way to have the most,
the most influential influence.
Did you ever have, in your years as a faculty member and or department head,
did you ever have a legislator or a regent attempt to intervene and twist your arm?
No, the closest I came was this little boy that gave the money
for the business administration bill.
What was his name?
John Blocker.
Blocker.
In the early 70s, we were having this energy.
And so I'd set up this energy symposium.
And I'd been down in Mexico, and I'd visited the old boy.
De La Pena was, he was in charge of all purchasing for PMAX.
In the last four years is where you rake in the money.
You know, you slip some out of all of it.
And let's see, I can't forget, remember the other guy's name.
He was in the University of Texas.
He was in charge of all production.
And so.
So when Blocker, for some reason, he heard about it.
So he sent his plane down to the King.
They stopped over at the King Ranch and went hunting down there a little bit, I think.
And then Blocker sent his plane over to pick them up.
And he also put them in the quarters for the Board of Regents over here at the MSC.
And then that night, he wanted to introduce the fellow from Texas.
He was in charge of all the oil.
And here's what he said.
He says, this is a great moment in history.
Mexico has just discovered they've got all of this oil.
And he said, they need to sell it.
And there's not a better person to sell it than I am.
That was the way he introduced it.
And it wasn't, well, a year or two after they were chasing both of those all over the country, they were wanting to know where they'd head out to.
Because.
Because they'd absconded with some money down there.
But that was one of the cases where I did have a Board of Directors member involved.
Actually, I knew Dr. Boyd, but not all that well.
We used to go fishing down there, and I'd talk to him.
He knew.
But I wasn't around him enough just to know him personally like I did Rudder.
All right, now, I've about finished questions.
Do you think of any topics that we should have talked about?
We didn't.
I don't.
It seems to me like we've covered an awful lot.
All right.
Let's see.
One item is to say if you have at this point or any time in the future any papers, memorabilia, or anything else that you are going to dispose of,
I beg you, please hand it over to the A&M Archives so it'll be preserved forever.
I think I've thrown about everything away already.
Well, that's what I worry about is things getting thrown away because it is the accumulation that can help give a more clear picture of A&M.
But, you know, we've had so little space at this place that we just didn't have room enough to store it.
But, see, now, over in the archives with the Cushing, they do.
Okay, next to last question.
Let's make believe that someone gives you a check for a million dollars, and it's a good, solid check,
and there's only one requirement.
You have to pass it on to Texas A&M, and you decide what they should do with it.
In other words, if you could give A&M a million dollars, what would you want them to do with it?
Oh, I think it should.
I'm not sure about other departments, but one of the places where we need money more than anywhere else,
and it's the hardest raised, and that is graduate fellowships.
You can raise money for undergraduate fellowships.
Because most people can associate with them that were undergraduates that have gone out and done well.
But the ones that's hardest is the graduate students and the, well, that one wouldn't get very far on chairs as far as that goes,
but I'd say with that amount of money that this would be helped with graduate fellowships
so that we could attract some real good students.
And I'd like to see them up the ante for USC.
Well, I'm not sure I know what you mean there.
That gives them a little better salary, and we do foreigners to encourage more of our own citizens to go to graduate school.
You're saying the stipend for graduate students.
The stipend for graduate students.
There's nothing in the world for us wrong with us being partial to our own students.
And so we don't have to treat them exactly like we treat the foreigners.
And so just to get some of ours in.
They are so up against it that they'll come here under a different set of circumstances, and our own people will.
But we want our own people in the, we want them involved.
And so we don't, in the industry and in the research, running the research organizations,
and so we need to get those kind of people in graduate school and not all foreigners.
Now, the Saudis didn't mind at all doing things like that because I was over there once,
and the king was having trouble with all the young Saudis.
The Saudis wanted to get out and drive automobiles up and down the beach.
And a lot of them got into driving cabs.
And so while I was there, he came out with a proclamation that no Saudi man could drive a cab until he was 30 years old.
He figured by then the fever would have worn off because they were looking at,
they wanted their people to go to get an education and help run that country.
And so I think we've got to do the same thing.
All right, last question.
After 50, more than 50 years of service here, what feelings do you have in your heart for Texas A&M?
Well, I think it's a great school, and I think that what we should have,
one of the things I see wrong with hiring so many, we always think we have to hire an outsider,
but a lot of times it's really I have a bag we'll travel.
And I think that we have, we need to hire an outsider.
And I think that we need more people coming up through organization like they have in companies
to where you know the culture and you have more dedicated people that will be,
do what they think is best for the university and not always career oriented what's best for me.
And so I think that's one of the things that I would say is that we do need more of that
so that we will get people who are dedicated and I think people have stayed with it a long time,
are dedicated and want to see the best for A&M.
